Tutorial Material: Block Matrix Tricks, Left/Right Inverses, and Moore-Penrose ​

Status: Material for future tutorials (not yet scheduled) Origin: Extracted from Week 5 draft (2026-03-25) to better align tutorial pacing with lecture progress across all sections. Prerequisites: Lectures 1–9 (through compatible projections), or at minimum Lectures 1–8.

Topic: The I−AB and I−BA Trick (10 min) ​

This is a beautiful block-matrix argument that connects I−AB and I−BA.

Setup: Let A be n×m and B be m×n. Consider three block matrices, each (n+m)×(n+m):

L1=(In−A0Im),M=(InABIm),L2=(In0−BIm)

Proposition

Identity 1

L1⋅M⋅L2=(In−AB00Im)

Verification (just matrix multiplication):

Step 1: L1⋅M:

(I−A0I)(IABI)=(I−AB0BI)

Step 2: multiply by L2:

(I−AB0BI)(I0−BI)=(I−AB00I)✓

Proposition

Identity 2 (swap roles of A and B)

L2⋅M⋅L1=(In00Im−BA)

Verification (exercise for students, same computation):

L2⋅M=(IA0I−BA), then multiply by L1 on the right. ✓

Attention

Key Observation

Both L1 and L2 are invertible: their inverses are

L1−1=(IA0I),L2−1=(I0BI)

(Verify: L1L1−1=I by direct multiplication.)

The identities say: the same matrix M is sandwiched between invertible factors to produce either I−AB or I−BA (up to block diagonal placement).

Proposition

Consequence: I−AB invertible ⟺ I−BA invertible

From Identity 1: (I−AB00I) is the product of L1, M, L2.

  • This product is invertible ⟺ all three factors are invertible ⟺ M is invertible (since L1,L2 are always invertible).

From Identity 2: (I00I−BA) is the product of L2, M, L1.

  • This product is invertible ⟺ M is invertible (same M!).

Now, the block diagonal (I−AB00I) is invertible ⟺ I−AB is invertible. (Its inverse is ((I−AB)−100I).)

Similarly, (I00I−BA) is invertible ⟺ I−BA is invertible.

Therefore:

I−AB invertible⟺M invertible⟺I−BA invertible◻

Example

Example

A=(12) (2×1), B=(31) (1×2).

AB=(3162) (2×2), so I2−AB=(−2−1−6−1), rank 2 → invertible ✓

BA=(3⋅1+1⋅2)=(5) (1×1), so I1−BA=(−4) → invertible ✓

Both invertible, as predicted. ✓

Remark

What if we replace I−AB by λI−AB?

Just replace A by 1λA (for λ≠0). Then:

λI−AB=λ(I−1λAB) invertible⟺I−1λAB invertible⟺I−BA1λ invertible⟺λI−BA invertible

So: λI−AB and λI−BA are invertible for the same values of λ. This will be important when we study eigenvalues in Chapter 5!

Topic: Left and Right Inverses (20 min) ​

Learning Goal ​

Students understand that left inverses exist for thin full-rank matrices, are not unique, and the choice of left inverse corresponds to a choice of subspace complement.

Suggested Approach ​

Step 1: When Do Inverses Exist? (3 min) ​

Attention

Key Principle

A left inverse of U is a matrix UL such that ULU=I.

Left inverse requires left cancelable: If U is not left cancelable, there exist X≠Y with UX=UY. But if a left inverse UL existed, then X=ULUX=ULUY=Y — contradiction.

Similarly, a right inverse of V (i.e., VVR=I) requires V to be right cancelable.

Summary:

  • Left inverse exists → U must be left cancelable (rank = columns), typically thin full rank
  • Right inverse exists → V must be right cancelable (rank = rows), typically fat full rank
  • Thin (not square) → has left inverse but no right inverse (since Col(UX)⊆Col(U)⊊Rm, so UX=Im is impossible)
  • Fat (not square) → has right inverse but no left inverse (dual argument)

Step 2: Constructing a Left Inverse (5 min) ​

Proposition

Construction

Let U be m×r with rank r. Choose any r×m matrix V such that VU is invertible. Then:

UL=(VU)−1V

is a left inverse of U.

Verification: ULU=(VU)−1V⋅U=(VU)−1(VU)=Ir. ✓

When is VU invertible? By the rank formula for VU: rank(VU)=r⟺Null(V)∩Col(U)={0}.

So we just need V whose null space doesn't overlap with Col(U).

Example

Example

U=(100111) (3×2, rank 2).

Choice 1: V1=(100010).

V1U=(1001)=I2 (already invertible!)

UL1=(I2)−1V1=V1=(100010)

Check: UL1U=I2 ✓. Note: Null(UL1)=span{(001)}.

Choice 2: V2=(10−101−1).

V2U=(0−1−10), (V2U)−1=(0−1−10)

UL2=(0−1−10)(10−101−1)=(0−11−101)

Check: UL2U=(0+0−1+1−1+00+1)=I2 ✓. Note: Null(UL2)=span{(111)}.

Two different left inverses! They differ because they have different null spaces.

Step 3: Uniqueness Given Null Space (7 min) ​

The example above shows: different null spaces → different left inverses. Is this the only source of non-uniqueness?

Proposition

Theorem: Unique Left Inverse Given Complement

Let U be m×r with rank r. Let W be a subspace of Rm that is a complement of Col(U), meaning:

  1. Col(U)∩W={0} (no overlap)
  2. Every vector in Rm can be written as v+w with v∈Col(U), w∈W

Then there is exactly one left inverse UL with Null(UL)=W.

Proof:

Suppose U1 and U2 are two left inverses of U with the same null space W.

Then (U1−U2)U=I−I=0.

Take any x∈Rm and decompose it: x=v+w where v∈Col(U) and w∈W.

On Col(U): Write v=Uc for some c. Then:

U1v=U1Uc=Ic=c=U2Uc=U2v

On W: Since both have null space W:

U1w=0=U2w

Combine: U1x=U1(v+w)=c+0=U2(v+w)=U2x.

This holds for every x, so U1=U2. ∎

Attention

One-to-One Correspondence

Left inverses of U⟷Complements of Col(U)

Each complement W determines exactly one left inverse (with null space W). Different complements give different left inverses.

Step 4: The Moore-Penrose Choice (5 min) ​

Among all complements of Col(U), there is one canonical choice: the orthogonal complement.

From Lecture 6: Col(U)⊥Null(UT), and their dimensions add up: r+(m−r)=m. So Null(UT) is a complement of Col(U) — the perpendicular one.

Proposition

The Moore-Penrose Left Inverse

The unique left inverse of U with null space Null(UT) is:

U+=(UTU)−1UT

This is called the (Moore-Penrose) pseudoinverse of U.

Why UTU is invertible: From the rank formula, rank(UTU)=r−dim⁡(Null(UT)∩Col(U)). But Col(U)⊥Null(UT) (Lecture 6), so Null(UT)∩Col(U)={0}. Therefore rank(UTU)=r, and UTU is invertible.

Verify it's a left inverse: U+U=(UTU)−1UTU=Ir ✓

Verify null space: U+w=(UTU)−1UTw. If w∈Null(UT), then UTw=0, so U+w=0. Therefore Null(UT)⊆Null(U+). Since both have dimension m−r (by cross-filling counting), they are equal. ✓

Example

Example

Same U=(100111).

UTU=(101011)(100111)=(2112)

(UTU)−1=13(2−1−12)

U+=13(2−1−12)(101011)=13(2−11−121)

Check: U+U=13(2+00−1−1+00+2)=I2 ✓

This left inverse is neither UL1 nor UL2 from the previous example — it's the one orthogonal to Col(U).

Challenging Problem ​

Example

Challenge: Finding a Basis of Col(U)∩Null(V)

Given: U=(100010001110) (4×3, rank 3), V=(10−10010−1) (2×4, rank 2)

Goal: Find a basis for Col(U)∩Null(V).

Method (from the rank formula proof):

Step 1: Compute VU:

VU=(10−10010−1)(100010001110)=(10−1−100)

Step 2: Find Null(VU) by cross-filling (VU∣0):

Pivot at (1,1): row (1,0,−1), column (1−1).

R1=11(1−1)(10−1)=(10−1−101)

Remainder: (000001)

Pivot at (2,3): gives equation x3=0.

From R1: x1−x3=0⇒x1=x3=0.

Free variable: x2. So Null(VU)=span{(010)}.

Step 3: Map through U:

U(010)=(0101)

Answer: Col(U)∩Null(V)=span{(0101)}.

Verify: (0101)∈Col(U)? Yes, U(010)=(0101) ✓

(0101)∈Null(V)? V(0101)=(00) ✓

Also: rank(VU)=3−1=2, confirmed by the cross-filling above. ✓

Connections to Future Material ​

  • Lecture 7–8 (Projections): UUL is a projection (P2=P). Different left inverses give different projections onto Col(U). The Moore-Penrose choice gives the orthogonal projection.
  • Lecture 8 (Theorem 4.4): Full rank square ⟺ invertible — the result referenced in the rank formula topic.
  • Lecture 10 (Constructing Projections): The formula P=B(AB)−1A is exactly the left-inverse construction in disguise.
  • Chapter 5 (Eigenvalues): The λI−AB / λI−BA equivalence means AB and BA share all nonzero eigenvalues.
